paragraphs.speaker: tim
paragraphs.name: Tim Ferriss
paragraphs.text: This episode is a two-for-one, because the podcast recently hit its 10-year anniversary and passed one billion downloads. To celebrate, I've curated some of the best of the best from more than 700 episodes over the last decade. My goal is to encourage you to enjoy the household names, but also introduce you to lesser-known people I consider stars. These are people who have transformed my life, and I feel like they can do the same for many of you.

paragraphs.speaker: jocko
paragraphs.name: Jocko Willink
paragraphs.text: The immediate answer that comes to mind is humility, because you've got to be humble and you've got to be coachable. We would fire guys later when I was running training, we would fire a couple leaders from every SEAL team because they couldn't lead. And 99.9% of the time, it wasn't a question of their ability, it was a question of their ability to listen and their ability to step outside and see that maybe there's a better way to do things.

paragraphs.speaker: jocko
paragraphs.name: Jocko Willink
paragraphs.text: I talk about the dichotomy of leadership. In a leadership situation, you're constantly balancing these opposing forces. Do you have to be aggressive? Absolutely. Can you be too aggressive? Yes. Do you need to be courageous? Yes. Can you be foolhardy and get people killed? Absolutely. Can you be too close to your men? Yes. Can you be not close enough? Yes. The best leaders have this ability to balance all those opposing forces.

paragraphs.speaker: jocko
paragraphs.name: Jocko Willink
paragraphs.text: A good leader would come back and say, I lost it. I didn't control it. I didn't do a good job. I got too absorbed in this little tiny tactical situation that was right in front of me. Either they'd make those criticisms themselves, or they'd say, what did I do wrong? And when you told them, they'd nod their head. They'd pull out their notebook. They'd take notes. That right there, that's a guy that's going to make it. Then you get the guy that comes in and immediately it's finger pointing. That's just a telltale sign you've got a guy that's not humble enough and coachable.

paragraphs.speaker: jocko
paragraphs.name: Jocko Willink
paragraphs.text: I call it detachment. Early on in my leadership career, I was probably 22 or 23 years old in my first SEAL platoon. We come up on this oil rig and everybody freezes. I said to myself, somebody's got to do something. So I just lifted my gun up toward the air and I took one step back off the line. I looked around and I saw what the picture was. And I just said, hold left, move right. And everybody heard it and they did it. And I said to myself, that's what you need to do. Detaching yourself from the situation so you can observe it is absolutely critical.

paragraphs.speaker: jocko
paragraphs.name: Jocko Willink
paragraphs.text: I wake up at 445. I like to have that psychological win over the enemy. When I wake up in the morning, I'm thinking about the enemy and what they're doing. And I know I'm not active duty anymore, but it's still in there that there's a guy that's in a cave somewhere and he's rocking back and forth. And he's got a machine gun in one hand and a grenade in the other hand. And he's waiting for me and we're going to meet. When I wake up in the morning, I'm thinking to myself, what can I do to be ready for that moment, which is coming?

paragraphs.speaker: jocko
paragraphs.name: Jocko Willink
paragraphs.text: One of my mantras is discipline equals freedom. I've found that the more disciplined you are, the more freedom you actually have. If you wake up early, you get more done, and you end up with more free time. The more disciplined you are physically with your diet, the more freedom you have because you can do more stuff. When a unit or a company is a disciplined group, they actually end up with more freedom. We had standard operating procedures for just about everything that we did. The more disciplined you are, the easier I could say, hey, you four, go take down that building, and they knew what to do.

paragraphs.speaker: sebastian
paragraphs.name: Sebastian Junger
paragraphs.text: I studied anthropology in college. After I got out, I wallowed around, I waited tables, I did various things to earn money while I was trying to become a writer. I got a job eventually as a climber for tree companies. I would work 80, 90 feet in the air with a chainsaw on a rope, taking trees down in pieces. It was extremely dangerous work. Or I should say, it's dangerous if you make a mistake. I realized at one point, if I get killed doing this, it will be because I killed myself by accident.
